title: He Kōrero Mō Te Awa: Voices of Our River / Ngā Reo o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural and environmental significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a vital waterway flowing through our rohe of Ngāti Kahungunu. They will engage with local pūrākau (stories) about the awa, learn about its historical importance as a food source and transport route, and examine current conservation efforts led by local kaitiaki.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret a local pūrākau (story) about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying its key themes and cultural values.
Compose a persuasive text (e.g., a speech, letter, or blog post) advocating for the protection of the awa, using evidence gathered from research and community interviews.
Collaborate effectively in a small group to create a digital story (e.g., a short video or podcast) that shares a community perspective on the awa's past, present, or future.
Use specific and descriptive language (precise nouns, verbs, adjectives, and figurative language) to vividly describe the awa and its environment in a piece of creative writing.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Pūrākau: A Walk and a Story
activities.description: The lesson begins with a guided hīkoi (walk) to the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. A local kaumātua or kaitiaki will share a pūrākau associated with the awa. Students will make initial observations and sketches of the environment. Back in class, they will work in pairs to identify the key values (e.g., kaitiakitanga, whanaungatanga, manaaki) and messages within the story.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero a te Iwi: Community Voice Interviews
activities.description: Students will develop interview questions to explore different perspectives on the awa. In small groups, they will interview a community member (e.g., a long-time resident, a farmer, a member of the local conservation group). They will practice active listening and note-taking skills to gather quotes and anecdotes for their final projects.

activities.title: He Kupu Whakaahua: Descriptive Writing Workshop
activities.description: Using their notes, sketches, and photos from the hīkoi, students will participate in a writing workshop. The focus will be on using precise and evocative language to describe the awa. They will explore using metaphors and similes inspired by the local environment (e.g., 'the water shimmered like pāua shell').

activities.title: Te Hāpai i te Reo Kaha: Crafting Persuasive Texts
activities.description: Drawing on their research, interviews, and descriptive writing, students will draft a persuasive text. They will learn about rhetorical devices and structure their writing to effectively argue for a specific action to protect or restore the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, supporting their claims with evidence.

activities.title: He Paki Waitara Matihiko: Digital Storytelling Project
activities.description: In their interview groups, students will synthesize their learning into a short digital narrative. This could be a video combining their footage, photos, and voice-over, or a podcast episode featuring interview clips. The project will tell a cohesive story about the awa from a community perspective.

resources: Local kaitiaki or kaumātua from the Mangakōtukutuku area (e.g., a representative from the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Care Group)
The digital story 'Te Awa Tupua' (https://www.ngatangitahi.com/projects/te-awa-tupua) as an exemplar
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources for environmental data
The book 'Hiakai: Modern Māori Cuisine' by Monique Fiso, which discusses traditional food gathering from waterways

tasks: A final persuasive text (500-600 words) advocating for the awa
A 3-4 minute digital story (video or podcast) presenting a community perspective
A written reflection on the process, connecting their learning to the concept of kaitiakitanga

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Health and Physical Education: Community resources; Attitudes and values
